1-5. If you drop a bird’s heart into water, it stands on the cut side and bears along its length a mark (as if) incised: the king will suffer an ailment of the [...]; for [its] “crossing over”: fit of madness; a terrible thing will be done in the palace.

6-10. If you drop a bird’s heart into water, it turns pale (and) bears a mark along its length: troops that we lead on campaign will come back safe. If (ditto): the king will grow old on his throne and not experience impoverishment at the hands of his enemy.

11-14. If I drop a bird’s heart into water (and) it is set in (a network of) white veins (as if) in a net for bundling straw: laḫbum-disease will strike the limbs of our troops and also the enemy’s weapon will overtake us. If (ditto): a sickness that will not let him go will take hold of the king.
15-17. If you drop a bird’s heart into water and […] along its length [it bears a mark ...]
1'-6' [If] I drop a bird’s heart into water and it is perforated from its right side to the left and is slit open(?), and also ...: on campaign ... will repulse me and ...
7'-12'. If [I] drop a bird’s heart into water and ...: ... will take from you.

13'–17' If [I] drop a bird’s heart into water and it throws up horns ... : on the campaign he undertakes the king will gore the lands like a wild bull. If (ditto): [the king] will remain seated [on] the throne; his guardian spirit [will not withdraw(?) from his side(?)] and his protective spirit [will not withdraw(?)] from [his] land.
19'–20' Hand of Šamaš-mušēšir, in the palace of King Tunip-Teššub. Month Ayyārum, 26th day, Qādiya being eponym.
